title.en: Birth Certificate Correction — Name, Date and Spelling Fixes, Step by Step

answerBox.en: Corrections to a birth certificate are made at the local registrar (municipality/panchayat) where the birth was registered: minor spelling/clerical fixes go on an application with documents and a small fee; substantive changes (name addition, parent details, date disputes) need an affidavit, supporting records, and sometimes a magistrate's order. Delayed registration (birth over 1 year old, never registered) now needs a District Magistrate's order.

en.label: Use everywhere after
en.startsFrom: birth certificate is now a single key document

en.title: Identify the correction type — it decides the route
en.summary: Clerical fix, name addition, or substantive change.
en.detail: Three lanes: **(1) clerical/typographical** (spelling of names, transposed letters) — registrar corrects on application with proof under **Section 15, Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969**; **(2) name addition** — where the child was registered unnamed, the name can be entered later within the rule-bound window; **(3) substantive changes** (changing the recorded date of birth, parent name disputes) — these face strict scrutiny, need strong contemporaneous evidence (hospital records, school records), affidavits, and often a **declaration from a magistrate/court**. Date-of-birth changes to gain age advantages are routinely refused.

en.title: Apply to the registrar who issued it
en.summary: Municipality/panchayat of the birthplace; many states online.
en.detail: File the correction application at the **registrar (municipal corporation/panchayat) where the birth was registered** — jurisdiction stays with the original registrar even if you live elsewhere now; several states accept online applications through their civil-registration portals (linked from **[crsorgi.gov.in](https://crsorgi.gov.in)**, the national CRS). Attach the original certificate, the proposed correction, and supporting documents: hospital discharge/birth record, parents' IDs, school records, vaccination card.

en.title: The affidavit + publication layer
en.summary: Most non-clerical corrections ride on a sworn affidavit.
en.detail: For name/spelling harmonisation and parent-detail fixes, registrars typically require a **notarised affidavit** from the parents (or the adult applicant) stating the error and the correct entry — our **₹199 service** drafts it to your municipality's format. Some states additionally want a newspaper publication for name-related corrections (akin to the [name change process](/help/family-personal/name-change-process)). Fees are small (₹25–200 typical, plus certificate copies).

en.title: Never registered, or registered late?
en.summary: The 2023 amendment tightened delayed registration — DM order.
en.detail: Births never registered: under the **RBD Amendment Act, 2023** regime, registration after one year requires an order of the **District Magistrate/sub-divisional magistrate** on verification of the birth (older law allowed a first-class magistrate's order; states are aligning procedures). Expect: application, non-availability certificate from the registrar, affidavit, school/hospital evidence, and the DM office verification. Since the birth certificate is now the single foundational document for school, passport, Aadhaar and licences, fixing gaps early saves compounding pain.

en.footnote: Keep every ID consistent after correction — mismatches between birth certificate, Aadhaar and school records are the root of most document rejections later in life.

en: birth certificate me naam kaise sudhare

en.q: Can I change the date of birth on a certificate?
en.a: Only with compelling contemporaneous evidence that the recorded date is wrong (hospital register, discharge summary) — registrars and courts refuse changes aimed at age advantage for jobs/school. Genuine clerical date errors supported by the hospital record do get corrected.

en.q: The birth was registered but no name was entered. How is the name added?
en.a: A born-unnamed entry can have the name entered later on the parents' application under the RBD rules — historically within 12–15 years. Take both parents' IDs, the certificate, and an affidavit; the registrar enters the name and issues a fresh certificate.

en.q: We've moved cities. Can the correction be done where I live now?
en.a: No — jurisdiction lies with the registrar where the birth was registered. Most states now accept online/postal applications so travel is often avoidable; an authorised representative with a specific power of attorney can also appear.

en.q: What if there is no birth certificate at all (born at home, never registered)?
en.a: Apply for delayed registration: non-availability certificate from the registrar, affidavit of the facts, supporting evidence (school records, vaccination card, ration/Aadhaar entries), and the District Magistrate's verification order under the current regime. It is paperwork-heavy but very doable — and worth doing before passport/visa needs force it under deadline.

en.q: Aadhaar/PAN/school records show a different spelling than the certificate. Which one do I fix?
en.a: Fix towards one authoritative spelling — usually the birth certificate corrected first, then Aadhaar and the rest aligned to it, since the 2023 amendments make the birth certificate the foundational document. A one-and-the-same-person affidavit bridges the gap during the transition.

reviewer: Adv. Priya Sharma, Bar Council of Delhi, D/1284/2016
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
